FULL CONTENT:
Published Time: Tue, 25 Jun 2024 23:48:22 GMT

# Language Barriers Among Immigrant Communities Language Barriers Among Immigrant Communities 

Rosia Aning 

Follow this and additional works at:  https://cedar.wwu.edu/wwu_honors 

Part of the  Other International and Area Studies Commons , and the  Social Justice Commons 

Recommended Citation Recommended Citation 

Aning, Rosia, "Language Barriers Among Immigrant Communities" (2023). 

WWU Honors College Senior 

Projects . 638. 

https://cedar.wwu.edu/wwu_honors/638 

This Project is brought to you for free and open access by the WWU Graduate and Undergraduate Scholarship at 

Western CEDAR. It has been accepted for inclusion in WWU Honors College Senior Projects by an authorized 

administrator of Western CEDAR. For more information, please contact  westerncedar@wwu.edu .Vocab Slide 2 

Immigrant: An immigrant is a person who moves from their home country to another country to 

live there permanently or for an extended period of time for a variety of reasons, such as seeking 

better economic opportunities, joining family members who already live in the new country, or 

escaping persecution or violence in their country of origin. Included within this group are 

individuals who have acquired citizenship through naturalization, individuals who have been 

granted permanent residency status, individuals who are in the country on student or work visas, 

as well as those who have been admitted under refugee or asylee status. 

Destination Country: Refers to the country to which a person or group of people are traveling 

or moving to with the intention of residing there either permanently or temporarily. It is the 

country of final destination for travelers or in case immigrants. 

Limited English Proficient: Limited English Proficient (LEP) refers to individuals who do not 

speak English as their primary language and have a limited ability to read, write, speak, or 

understand English. 

Slide 3 

The chart in the slide shows the number of immigrants who have been living in the United 

States, as well as their percentage relative to the overall population of the country, over a specific 

time period. This data can provide valuable insights into the social and economic implications of 

immigration in the United States, such as how it affects cultural diversity and the workforce. By 

examining this data, one can observe trends and changes in the immigrant population in the 

United States, which can help in understanding the impact of immigration over time. 

Around 20.7 million immigrants, which is roughly about 46 percent of the total 44.6 million 

immigrants aged 5 and older in the United States in 2019, had limited proficiency in the English 

language (LEP). The immigrant population accounted for a significant portion of the country's 

LEP population, making up 81 percent of the 25.5 million individuals with LEP in the United 

States. These statistics highlight the importance of providing language assistance services to the 

immigrant population to ensure that they can access and understand essential information such as 

medical or legal advice, educational materials, and public services. Slide 4: 

In 2021, 78 percent or roughly 245.5 million residents aged 5 and above in the United States 

reported speaking solely English at home, regardless of their place of birth. The remaining 22 

percent, comprising approximately 67.8 million individuals, reported using a language other than 

English at home. Of those who reported speaking a non-English language at home, 61 percent 

spoke Spanish, followed by Chinese (including Mandarin and Cantonese) at about 5 percent, 

Tagalog at almost 3 percent, and Vietnamese, Arabic, French (including Cajun), and Korean at 

about 2 percent. These figures reveal the linguistic diversity of the US population, highlighting 

the importance of providing language assistance and support to those who need it to ensure 

equitable access to information, services, and resources. 

Slide 5 

The chart shows that with the exception of three states, Hawaii, Maine, and Vermont (French), 

Spanish was the most widely spoken language, apart from English, at home in all other US 

states. Additionally, other languages besides English and Spanish that were commonly spoken at 

home included Chinese, German, French, and Vietnamese. These findings highlight the linguistic 

diversity of the United States and emphasize the importance of promoting multilingualism and 

providing language assistance services to ensure that individuals who speak languages other than 

English or Spanish can access essential information and services. 

Slide 6 - Challenges Immigrants Face In Destination Countries 

●

Discrimination and prejudice : Immigrants may face discrimination and prejudice in 

their destination countries, which can make it difficult to feel welcomed and accepted in 

their new home. 

● Employment and economic challenges : Finding employment and economic stability is 

often a challenge for immigrants in destination countries. 

● Cultural adjustment: Adjusting to a new culture can be challenging for immigrants. 

They may struggle with different customs, values, and social norms, which can impact 

their ability to integrate into the community and form meaningful relationships. ● Legal and administrative challenges: Immigrants may face legal and administrative 

challenges in their destination countries. This can include navigating complex 

immigration systems, dealing with paperwork and bureaucracy, and understanding their 

rights and obligations under the law. 

● Language barriers: Also one of the biggest challenges immigrants face in destination 

countries 

Slide 7 

This is a YouTube video about the responses obtained from asking immigrants with diverse 

backgrounds to share their perspectives on “Speak English” to reveal the complexity and 

uniqueness of human experiences and viewpoints. This video shows a greater appreciation and 

understanding of human diversity and promotes empathy and respect for others' experiences and 

perspectives. 

Slide 8 - Linguistic Human Rights and Why They Are Important 

1. The right to speak, read, write, and otherwise use one's language of choice without 

interference or discrimination from others. 

2. The right to use one's language in public and private settings, including in education, 

government, media, and commerce. 

3. The right to receive public services and information in one's language of choice, and to 

have access to translations or interpretations when necessary. 

4. The right to maintain and develop one's language, including through education, media, 

and cultural institutions. 

5. The right to use one's language in legal proceedings and other official contexts, including 

the right to an interpreter or translator if necessary. 

Language rights are considered an important aspect of human rights, as language is often central 

to one's identity, culture, and social participation. The presence of language barriers presents 

major obstacles for immigrant communities on a global scale. Immigrants who relocate to a new 

country often encounter hurdles in communicating with native speakers due to linguistic 

differences. This inability to communicate effectively can result in a wide range of negative 

consequences, such as restricted access to essential resources and information, economic disadvantage, social exclusion, and disparities in healthcare. I will be talking about the diverse 

ways in which language barriers affect immigrant communities and propose potential strategies 

that could help this issue. 

Slide 10 - Negative Impact of Language Barriers 

1. Education: 

One of the most significant ways in which language barriers affect immigrant communities is in 

the area of education. Children from immigrant families who speak limited English may struggle 

to understand classroom instruction, communicate with teachers and peers, and participate fully 

in school activities. This can lead to poor academic performance and limited opportunities for 

social and developmental growth. In addition, schools may struggle to provide targeted support 

and services to students who speak multiple languages, especially in communities with a high 

concentration of non-native English speakers. Schools may lack the resources to offer specialized 

language instruction or hire translators to communicate with parents who do not speak English 

fluently. As a result, students may not receive the support they need to succeed academically. 

In Finding Courage In The Unfamiliar , Japhety tells a story about the hardships he faced with 

his family when they moved to the United States. He was born in a refugee camp in Tanzania 

where his family had fled to escape the civil war in Burundi, East Africa. His family immigrated 

to the US in 2009 and he talks about the hardships he faced in school and how he had to adapt to 

a lot of things. In this story, he mentions that after completing his first year of school in the 

United States, he was unable to progress to the third grade due to language barriers, resulting in 

him being held back to repeat the second grade. Though initially frustrated, he was determined to 

prove his academic capabilities to himself and excel in the classroom. Due to the language 

barrier, he may have felt isolated from his classmates and unable to fully engage in classroom 

activities. Overall, being held back in school due to language barriers can be a challenging and 

discouraging experience for students, especially those who are determined to succeed 

academically. 

Slide 11 

2. Employment: ● Workers who do not speak the language of their host country fluently may struggle to 

find work, particularly in fields that require strong communication skills. 

● Employers may be hesitant to hire workers who do not speak the language of their 

workplace fluently. This is because they may be concerned about communication 

difficulties or misunderstandings that could lead to errors or conflicts. As a result, 

immigrants may face discrimination during the hiring process and have limited 

opportunities to secure meaningful employment. 

● Immigrants who do not speak the language of their host country fluently may be limited 

to jobs that require minimal communication or interaction with others, such as manual 

labor or cleaning jobs. These jobs often pay lower wages and offer limited opportunities 

for career advancement. 

● Workers who do not speak the language of their workplace fluently may not be able to 

understand important information about their rights as employees, such as minimum 

wage laws, safety regulations, or workplace policies. This lack of understanding can 

make it difficult for them to advocate for themselves or seek help if they are mistreated or 

exploited by their employer. 

Slide 12 

3. Healthcare: 

● Language barriers can limit access to quality healthcare for immigrants as patients who 

do not speak the language of their healthcare providers fluently may struggle to 

comprehend important medical information, such as medical instructions or prescriptions. 

● It creates difficulties in accessing healthcare services. Immigrants who do not speak the 

language of their host country fluently may find it challenging to navigate the healthcare 

system, such as making appointments or filling out paperwork. This can lead to delays in 

receiving medical care, which can have serious consequences for their health. 

● Moreover, cultural misunderstandings due to language barriers can also impact the 

quality of healthcare provided to immigrants. Healthcare providers who are not familiar 

with the cultural norms and expectations of the patient's community may not provide 

culturally sensitive care, leading to miscommunications or misunderstandings that can 

impact the quality of care provided. Slide 13 

4. Social Integration: 

● Language rights are essential for immigrants to access information and services in their 

native language, which can help them participate in their host society and reduce social 

isolation. When immigrants are able to access information and services in their native 

language, they are better equipped to participate in their host society. This can help to 

reduce social isolation and promote a sense of belonging within the community. 

● On the other hand, language barriers can prevent immigrants from fully engaging with 

their host society, leading to social isolation and the formation of ethnic enclaves. Ethnic 

enclaves are neighborhoods or communities where immigrants cluster together, and they 

can limit opportunities for interaction with members of the wider community. 

Slide 14 

5. Family Life: 

● When parents and children do not share a common language, it can create communication 

difficulties and misunderstandings, leading to potential conflicts and frustrations. 

Children may be forced to take on the role of translators for their parents, which can put 

pressure on them and may interfere with their own academic and social development. 

This can also create a reversal of roles where the children become the primary 

decision-makers, which can lead to a breakdown in traditional family structures. The 

story linked in this slide is another immigrant story where Belise from Tanzania mentions 

that her older sister Serapiya, who was only 8 years old when her family moved to the 

United States was burdened with adult responsibilities like filling out school forms, 

paperwork, and translating for her family. This can be a frustrating experience for a child, 

as they may feel overwhelmed and forced to grow up too quickly. It can also create 

additional pressure on the child to perform well and meet the expectations of their family, 

which can lead to stress and anxiety. Being responsible for tasks that are typically 

performed by adults can affect a child's ability to focus on their own development and 

education. Therefore, while it is admirable for a child like Serapiya to take on adult responsibilities, it can be a difficult and challenging experience that may have long-term 

consequences. 

● It can create challenges in accessing resources and services for the family, particularly in 

areas such as education and healthcare. For instance, parents may face difficulties in 

communicating with teachers or understanding school policies and procedures, which can 

negatively impact their children's academic performance. Similarly, parents may struggle 

to navigate the healthcare system or access medical services for their children due to 

language barriers, which can have significant impacts on the health and well-being of the 

family. 

● It can also make it challenging for immigrant families to preserve their cultural traditions 

and pass them down to the next generation. Suppose parents and children do not share a 

common language. In that case, it can be difficult for them to communicate and 

participate in cultural practices together, leading to a potential loss of cultural heritage 

and identity. This can be particularly difficult for immigrant families, who may already 

need help with adapting to a new cultural environment. 

Slide 15 - Potential Solution 

1. To address language barriers among immigrant communities, there are several potential 

solutions that can be implemented. One approach is to provide language training for 

immigrants, which can help them develop the skills they need to communicate effectively 

in their destination country. This training can be provided by schools, community 

organizations, or government agencies, and can be tailored to meet the unique needs of 

different communities. 

2. Another potential solution to language barriers is the provision of translation and 

interpretation services. Schools, healthcare providers, and government agencies can offer 

translation and interpretation services to ensure that everyone has access to the 

information they need to fully participate in society. The range of languages available 

should be as diverse as possible to cater to the different languages spoken by the 

immigrants. 

3. Employing bilingual staff can also be an effective way to ensure that immigrant 

communities have access to effective communication with service providers. This can be particularly important in healthcare, where effective communication can impact health 

outcomes. Hiring bilingual staff can also help to create a more diverse and inclusive 

workplace, which can improve the quality of care or services provided. 

4. Cultural competency training is another potential solution to language barriers. Training 

healthcare providers, educators, and other service providers in cultural competency can 

help them to better understand the needs and expectations of immigrant communities. 

This can lead to more effective and sensitive care and support, and help to overcome 

cultural misunderstandings that can arise due to language barriers. 

5. Civil and community organizations can work to promote and protect linguistic human 

rights for all individuals, regardless of their ethnicity or background. This can include 

implementing policies that promote diversity and inclusion, providing language services 

to communities that need them, and investing in education and resources for immigrant 

communities. Community organizations can play an important role in providing support 

and resources to immigrant families. They can provide language training for both 

children and adults and work to connect families with resources such as tutoring, 

mentoring, and after-school programs. 

6. Immigrant communities can also advocate for their own rights with the support of allies 

Slide 16

one is watching.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Immigrants face significant language barriers, impacting their access to healthcare and social services, as limited proficiency restricts effective communication and understanding of vital information.
Cultural norms may discourage individuals from seeking help, leading to underutilization of available support services, as seeking assistance can be perceived as a sign of weakness.

Differences in cultural values between generations can create conflicts in support dynamics, as younger generations may adopt more progressive views, challenging traditional norms upheld by older family members.
Stigmatization of seeking help, particularly in immigrant communities, can prevent individuals from accessing necessary support, as they fear judgment or discrimination from their peers.
Culturally competent care is crucial for effectively addressing the unique needs of diverse populations, ensuring that services are respectful, relevant, and tailored to individual cultural contexts.
